3A_Bibliographic.citation: Fors, P. Q., & Barch, D. M. (2019). Differential relationships of child anxiety and depression to child report and parent report of electronic media use. Child Psychiatry & Human Development, 50, 907-917.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Not explicitly named; data obtained through NDAR/NIMH Data Archive
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Data from a sample of 4524 children in the ABCD study were used. Due to missing values on some measures, the sample size for the current analysis was 4139 children. Participants were excluded from analysis if they or their parent/guardian did not complete a questionnaire, or if they answered, 'do not know' for questions. The included participants were 8.9-11 years of age (mean=10 years, SD=0.61).
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Uses early ABCD data release from NIMH Data Archive

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Electronic media use (EMU) - child report weekday/weekend and parent report weekday/weekend total minutes
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) - child version (14 items) and parent version (2 items)
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV shows/movies, videos, video games, texting, social networks, video chatting (child report); total media use on weekdays and weekends (parent report)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (standardized total minutes for weekday and weekend, child and parent report)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The ABCD Screen Time Survey (STQ) was completed by the children, and a different, shorter STQ was completed by the child's parent/guardian. The STQ for children is a 14-item questionnaire that asks how many hours per day a child uses different forms of electronic media... The STQ for parents is a 2-item questionnaire that asks about the child's total media use on weekdays and on weekends, as a continuous variable in minutes.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Child anxiety (CBCL anxiety subscale T-score) and child depression (CBCL depression subscale T-score)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) - anxiety subscale (9 items) and depression subscale (13 items), parent-reported
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Parents/guardians completed the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL), a 112-item questionnaire that asks about various symptoms and behaviors the adolescent may exhibit. The CBCL contains 9 statements used to form an anxiety subscale... The CBCL was also used to measure depression symptoms in children, a subscale containing 13 statements... T-scores were calculated and used for analysis.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Generalized linear mixed models with testing site and family as nesting factors, standardized variables
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, BMI, family income, race, physical activity
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: All remaining analyses used generalized linear mixed models, and accounted for nesting within testing site and family, and controlled for age, sex, BMI, family income, race, and physical activity... Of note, we standardized all variables in the analyses presented below to allow clearer interpretation of the coefficients in terms of effect sizes. The significance level for all tests was 0.05.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Child reported weekday use - Anxiety: Coefficient 0.055, t=3.309, Sig.=0.001, -95%CI=0.022, +95%CI=0.088
Table 2: Child reported weekend use - Anxiety: Coefficient 0.060, t=3.689, Sig.=<0.001
Table 2: Parent reported weekend use - Anxiety: Coefficient 0.070, t=4.255, Sig.=<0.001
Table 2: Child reported weekday use - Depression: Coefficient 0.050, t=3.026, Sig.=0.002
Table 2: Parent reported weekend use - Depression: Coefficient 0.084, t=5.158, Sig.=<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Standardized coefficient; not significant when controlling for depression
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Child reported weekday use - Anxiety accounting for depression: Coefficient 0.026, t=1.955, Sig.=0.051

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Standardized coefficient; still significant when controlling for anxiety
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Child reported weekend use - Depression accounting for anxiety: Coefficient 0.032, t=2.427, Sig.=0.015

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: accounting for depression significantly reduced the relationship between electronic media use and anxiety
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: while our findings were significant, the effect sizes were small, with standardized beta weights in the range of 0.05 to 0.1. Thus, while we did find significant associations, the magnitude of these associations in this large population-based sample were small.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper explicitly notes small effect sizes and constrains conclusions appropriately. One of the more measured papers in the review.

3H_Recommendations.notes: Paper does not issue clinical/policy recommendations. Discussion focuses on scientific interpretation, acknowledges small effects, and calls for future longitudinal research.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion presents a nuanced interpretation. It finds that after controlling for depression, none of the EMU measures significantly predicted anxiety, suggesting previous EMU-anxiety findings may reflect comorbidity with depression. Depression associations with EMU were more robust. The paper explicitly notes small effect sizes (betas 0.05-0.10) and constrains interpretations accordingly. It avoids clinical recommendations and focuses on calling for future longitudinal research to examine causal relationships.
